(7) Step-by-Step Docker — Building a Docker Image
We've covered a fair bit on Docker commands so far. This post walks through actually building a Docker Image.

So far we've covered Docker Hub commands and Docker Container commands, but haven't really gotten into Docker Images themselves — even though we mentioned a Docker Image is what a Container is built from.

How do you build your own Docker Image? It's the Dockerfile we covered earlier. The Dockerfile is what produces a Docker Image. Earlier we only walked through Dockerfile syntax without actually packaging anything. This post is where we do that.
For this, we need something to package into an Image. I'll use Express.js as the example. If you don't have an environment set up, you can clone
Your project should look like this:
Let's create the Dockerfile. I'll use VSCode — if you don't have it, install it
In the project root, create a file called
What goes in it? Usually the first line is
because we need to specify the environment Express will run in. Let's go with Node 16.15.0:
language-dockerfile shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
Then we typically set up a working directory — usually
After installing deps, set an env var for the port — we'll use 3000:
Then tell the Docker Container which port it'll be using:
And the command to actually run on startup —

FROM node:16.15.0

WORKDIR /app
COPY . .

ENV PORT=3000

RUN ["npm","install"]

EXPOSE 3000

CMD ["npm","start"]
Way simpler than the example in the day-2 post, right?
, so let me explain why we need a working directory and why we copy everything into it.
By default, Docker runs commands at the root directory
. That causes issues — when you run terminal commands, they're all running at root. If you skip creating a working directory and copy your files straight to root, weird things happen.
So you create a working directory and copy your files there to avoid that.
Dockerfile is done. Let's build the Image.
Before building, double-check the Dockerfile is correct — typos and syntax errors will bite you.
To build, cd into the directory containing the Dockerfile and run:
language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
, and the Dockerfile is in the current directory
Docker will start building. This takes a while — be patient.
Once done, list your Images with:
Creating a Container and running it
Now let's convert that Image into a Container and run it. Our Image is
. Note: you need to specify both Image name and tag, because Docker lists all same-named Images otherwise.
maps a port — it connects the Container's port to your host machine's port, so you can access the Express server inside the Container from your host.
runs the Container in the background ("detached"), so your terminal isn't blocked. Without
, your terminal hangs until the Container exits, like this:
, you can't access the Express server inside the Container.
You've now built and run your own Docker Image. If you want to see Express's API request logs inside the Container, run:
This tails the Container's logs in real time.
See you in the next post.
